#### Insight: Stack

A Stack is a fundamental LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure. It mimics a physical stack of plates where elements are added (Push) and removed (Pop) only from the Top. Internally, it can be implemented using arrays or linked lists. It is crucial for expression parsing, depth-first search, and managing function calls in the OS kernel. Key operations take O(1) time. Overflow occurs when the fixed size is exceeded, while Underflow happens on pop from an empty stack.

#### Key Points:
* O(1) Operations
* Critical System Component
* Robust Multi-Language Support

#### Insight: Linked List

Linked Lists are dynamic data structures consisting of nodes, where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node. Unlike arrays, they don't require contiguous memory and can grow/shrink easily. Operations like insertion and deletion at the head are O(1), but searching takes O(n). Types include Singly, Doubly (pointers in both directions), and Circular (last node points to first). Memory management is critical to avoid leaks.

#### Insight: Graph

Graphs represent relationships between objects (Nodes) via Edges. They can be Directed (one-way) or Undirected (mutual). Traversal is performed using Breadth-First Search (BFS) for layer-by-layer exploration or Depth-First Search (DFS) for path-based deep dives. BFS uses a Queue and is ideal for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs, while DFS uses a Stack (or recursion) and is used for topological sorting or cycle detection. Complexity depends on V (vertices) and E (edges), typically O(V+E).

#### Key Points:
* High Algorithmic Density
* Optimal Scaling
* Multi-Language Core Logic

#### Insight: Graph

Graphs represent relationships between objects (Nodes) via Edges. They can be Directed (one-way) or Undirected (mutual). Traversal is performed using Breadth-First Search (BFS) for layer-by-layer exploration or Depth-First Search (DFS) for path-based deep dives. * Optimal Scaling
* Multi-Language Core Logic

#### Insight: Dijkstra

Dijkstra's Algorithm finds the shortest path from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It uses a Greedy approach, maintaining a set of shortest-reach distances and a priority queue to pick the closest unvisited node. Its complexity is O((V+E) log V) with a binary heap. It is widely used in network routing and GPS navigation systems. Note: It fails on graphs with negative weights.

#### Key Points:
* High Algorithmic Density
* Optimal Scaling
* Multi-Language Core Logic

#### Insight: Merge Sort

Merge Sort is a stable, divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm. It recursively splits the array into halves until single elements remain, then merges them back in sorted order. It guarantees O(n log n) time complexity in all cases, making it highly reliable for large datasets. However, it requires O(n) auxiliary space for the merging process. It is preferred for linked list sorting due to its sequential access patterns.

#### Key Points:
* High Algorithmic Density
* Optimal Scaling
* Multi-Language Core Logic

Traversal is performed using Breadth-First Search (BFS) for layer-by-layer exploration or Depth-First Search (DFS) for path-based deep dives. BFS uses a Queue and is ideal for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs, while DFS uses a Stack (or recursion) and is used for topological sorting or cycle detection. Complexity depends on V (vertices) and E (edges), typically O(V+E).

* Multi-Language Core Logic

#### Insight: Merge Sort

Merge Sort is a stable, divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm. It recursively splits the array into halves until single elements remain, then merges them back in sorted order. It guarantees O(n log n) time complexity in all cases, making it highly reliable for large datasets. However, it requires O(n) auxiliary space for the merging process. It is preferred for linked list sorting due to its sequential access patterns.

#### Insight: Dijkstra

Dijkstra's Algorithm finds the shortest path from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It uses a Greedy approach, maintaining a set of shortest-reach distances and a priority queue to pick the closest unvisited node. Its complexity is O((V+E) log V) with a binary heap. It is widely used in network routing and GPS navigation systems. Note: It fails on graphs with negative weights.

#### Insight: Hash Map

A Hash Map (or Hash Table) stores key-value pairs and uses a Hash Function to compute an index in an array. This allows for near constant-time O(1) average lookup, insertion, and deletion. Collisions (when two keys hash to the same index) are handled via Chaining (linked lists at indices) or Open Addressing (finding next available slots). Performance depends heavily on the quality of the hash function and the load factor of the table.

#### Key Points:
* High Performance Retrieval
* Efficient Memory Mapping
* Standard Technical Foundation

#### Insight: Quick Sort

Quick Sort is a highly efficient, in-place sorting algorithm based on the divide-and-conquer principle. It selects a 'pivot' element and partitions the array such that elements smaller than the pivot are on the left and larger on the right. It then recursively sorts the sub-arrays. While its worst-case is O(n²), its average-case is O(n log n) with a very small constant factor, often outperforming Merge Sort in practice. Randomized pivot selection is commonly used to avoid the worst-case scenario.

#### Key Points:
* High Performance Retrieval
* Efficient Memory Mapping
* Standard Technical Foundation

A Hash Map (or Hash Table) stores key-value pairs and uses a Hash Function to compute an index in an array. This allows for near constant-time O(1) average lookup, insertion, and deletion. Collisions (when two keys hash to the same index) are handled via Chaining (linked lists at indices) or Open Addressing (finding next available slots). Performance depends heavily on the quality of the hash function and the load factor of the table.

#### Insight: Quick Sort

Quick Sort is a highly efficient, in-place sorting algorithm based on the divide-and-conquer principle. It selects a 'pivot' element and partitions the array such that elements smaller than the pivot are on the left and larger on the right. It then recursively sorts the sub-arrays. While its worst-case is O(n²), its average-case is O(n log n) with a very small constant factor, often outperforming Merge Sort in practice. Randomized pivot selection is commonly used to avoid the worst-case scenario.
